clean_story:
Five gateways to the world. Each rewires reality.

The word sense comes from Latin sentire—"to feel" or "perceive." But senses don’t just passively receive; they edit. Your eyes flip images upside-down, yet your brain flips them back without you noticing. Your nose detects a trillion smells but only lets you name a handful, like recognizing a face in a crowd of strangers.

Here’s how it works: Light hits your retina like pixels on a screen, but your brain fills gaps—turning blurry edges into sharp lines. Sound waves vibrate tiny hairs in your ears, each tuned to a specific pitch, like a piano’s strings. Touch isn’t skin-deep; your fingertips read Braille at 400 dots per second, faster than a scanner. Taste buds detect five basics (sweet, salty, sour, bitter, umami), but texture and temperature trick you into "tasting" spice as heat. Pain isn’t just damage—it’s a warning system, like a car’s check-engine light.

Loss sharpens the rest: Blindfolded people hear better within days. Deaf musicians feel vibrations as melody. Even your "sixth sense"—proprioception—lets you touch your nose with closed eyes, no mirrors needed.

Three related subjects are synesthesia’s crossed wires, animal senses beyond humans, and how VR hijacks perception.
